title: Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita

GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between government expenditure per primary school student as a percentage of GDP per capita and the GDP per capita itself for various countries in 2008. The vertical axis represents the share of GDP per capita spent, ranging from 0% to over 40%, while the horizontal axis shows GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, plotted on a logarithmic scale from $2,000 to $100,000. Countries are color-coded by region: North America (orange), South America (maroon), Africa (purple), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue). Serbia stands out with the highest expenditure share, exceeding 40% of its GDP per capita. Djibouti follows with a share around 38%. Several European nations, including Albania, Ukraine, and Cyprus, cluster between 30% and 35%. A large group of countries, including Thailand, Latvia, Malta, Austria, and Australia, fall within the 20% to 25% range. Many African nations like Burundi, Niger, and Rwanda, along with some Asian countries such as Cambodia and India, are positioned below the 15% mark. Kazakhstan exhibits one of the lowest shares, near 0%. The distribution shows no clear linear trend; countries with both low and high GDP per capita can be found across various expenditure share levels. For instance, high-income countries like Bermuda and Hong Kong have shares around 10-15%, similar to lower-income countries like Guinea or Afghanistan.
